label: DMM.B5.1
text: Agallochon (Aloewood / Aquilaria) — ἀγάλλοχον — Indian wood, also called agallochon or fragrant wood. It is warming, digestive, strengthens the mouth and stomach, is good for those with liver conditions when decocted in wine. Applied to the forehead with rose oil it helps headache. Burned as incense it is pleasant and aromatic.

label: DMM.B5.2
text: Maron (Wine of Maronea — Thrace) — The wine of Maronea mentioned in Homer's Odyssey when Odysseus gave it to the Cyclops. So strong that it was mixed with twenty measures of water to one of wine. Used medicinally: warms the stomach, promotes digestion, increases urine, helps those with phlegm. The strongest wines are most warming and have most power.

label: DMM.B5.3
text: Absinthites Oinos (Wormwood Wine) — ἀψινθίτης οἶνος — Wormwood wine. Wormwood leaves and flowers steeped in grape must. Taken in drink it warms the stomach and helps those with fluxes of the belly. Good for those with jaundice and for those who have been made ill by drinking hemlock. One of the most widely prescribed medicinal wines.

label: DMM.B5.4
text: Scillites Oinos (Squill Wine) — σκιλλίτης οἶνος — Squill wine made from sea squill (Urginea maritima). Given for coughs and chest conditions, for those with longstanding phlegm in the chest, for dropsy (edema), and for the bite of vipers. Prepared by soaking dried squill root in must for 40 days. Squill contains cardiac glycosides — the digitalis-like compounds in this tradition. Extremely potent: used as both medicine and rat poison in antiquity.

label: DMM.B5.5
text: Rhodites Oinos (Rose Wine) — ῥοδίτης οἶνος — Rose wine, made by macerating rose petals in must for one year. Cooler than plain wine. Good for those with stomach conditions, headache, and conditions of the mouth and gums. Applied to wounds it cools inflammation. The most widely prescribed medicinal wine in Greek/Roman tradition — rose wine as a base for other medicinal preparations.

label: DMM.B5.6
text: Oxymeli (Honey-Vinegar) — ὀξύμελι — Honey and vinegar preparation. The best is made from vinegar and Attic honey boiled together. Given for those with coughs, for those with bile conditions, for those with palpitations of the heart. Mixed with water it purges the belly. Applied externally it helps those with conditions of the joints. The oxymeli preparation is the Greek/Roman equivalent of later European oxymel — still used in herbal medicine today.

label: DMM.B5.7
text: Melikraton (Hydromel / Mead) — μελίκρατον — Old water mixed with honey. The best honey dissolves in the purest water and is boiled down. Taken in drink it is laxative, helps those with coughs, clears the chest. Applied to wounds it cleanses them. Used as a vehicle for other medicinal plants — honey's antimicrobial properties serving as a preservative medium for plant medicines. Convergence with Egyptian medicine: honey as wound treatment appears in the Ebers Papyrus (c.1550 BCE), Greek/Roman medicine, Ayurveda, and virtually every traditional system.

label: DMM.B5.8
text: Dioscorides on mineral medicines — Lithos haimatites — λίθος αἱματίτης — Bloodstone (hematite, iron ore). Ground finely and taken in drink with wine it stops fluxes of the belly and uterine hemorrhage. Applied to the eyes with egg white it reduces inflammation. Ground finely and applied to wounds it draws the lips together. The iron content provides a pharmacological basis for the hemostatic claim — iron salts have documented coagulating properties.

label: DMM.B5.9
text: Alum (Stypteria) — στυπτηρία — Alum. The best is the roundish kind from Egypt. It has drawing, drying, astringent properties. Dissolved in warm water and gargled it helps conditions of the mouth and tonsils. Applied to granulating wounds it helps them close. Mixed with honey it is applied to foul ulcers. Applied in vinegar to running sores it heals them. One of the most universally used mineral antiseptics across all ancient traditions.

label: DMM.B5.10
text: Chrysokolla (Borax / Malachite) — χρυσόκολλα — Gold-solder, the material used by goldsmiths. Applied to the eyes it clears the vision. Applied to wounds it helps them close. Taken with water it is bitter and causes vomiting. The identification of chrysokolla with specific modern minerals is debated — possibly borax, malachite, or azurite depending on context. Documents the early use of mineral medicines alongside plant medicines.

label: DMM.B6.1
text: Mekon (White Poppy — alternative entry) — μήκων λευκή — White poppy. The juice of the heads, dried, is called opion. Of lesser strength than the black kind. The milk from the heads taken in drink is soporific. The seeds taken with wine and honey provoke sleep and stop fluxes of the belly. The heads boiled in water and the liquid applied to the forehead stops headache. Note on dose: a little helps, more is lethal. Extended entry on the pharmacological gradations of opium from soporific to lethal.

label: DMM.B6.2
text: Hyoskyamos niger (Black Henbane — extended entry) — ὑοσκύαμος ὁ μέλας — Black henbane. The leaves of all kinds, taken in excess, cause madness and loss of reason. The seed drunk in wine causes hallucinations and deep sleep. Applied to the eyes with woman's milk it relieves pain. The root boiled in wine and held in the mouth relieves toothache. ANTIDOTES: If someone has drunk too much henbane, give them honeyed wine (oxymel) or decoction of radish in water to vomit it out. This antidote section demonstrates Greek/Roman awareness of both the therapeutic and toxic doses.

label: DMM.B6.3
text: Mandragoras (Mandrake — extended toxic-therapeutic entry) — μανδραγόρας — Mandrake. The root beaten with olive oil and applied is good for wounds that have been made frostbitten. Boiled in wine and applied it helps those with gout, with conditions of the joints, and those bitten by erysipelas. The root prepared as a wine: take one mina of root to one amphora of must, let stand for three months. Given before operations and cauterisations to produce insensibility to pain — the first documented surgical anesthetic protocol in Western medical literature. Those who drink more than the due measure are made speechless forever. SAFETY: When preparing the wine, the physician must stand to the windward side of the plant to avoid inhaling the fumes.

label: DMM.B6.4
text: Konion (Hemlock — Socrates' poison) — κώνειον — Hemlock. The whole plant is cold and narcotic. The juice of the plant applied to the testicles before puberty prevents them from growing and stops desire. Applied to the breasts of girls before they develop it prevents the breasts from growing. The ancient physicians used hemlock to prevent excessive breast development. But taken in drink hemlock kills. It kills by making the body cold from below upward until it reaches the heart and lungs. ANTIDOTE: Give warm wine and make the patient walk until sleep overcomes them — keep them awake if possible. Historical note: The death of Socrates (399 BCE) as described by Plato in the Phaedo matches Dioscorides' description of hemlock poisoning exactly — numbness ascending from feet to heart.

label: DMM.B6.5
text: Akonition (Aconite / Wolfsbane) — ἀκόνιτον — Aconite (wolfsbane). This plant is of all things the swiftest to kill. It kills by chilling. Given to leopards and wild beasts smeared on meat it kills them quickly — hence it is called leopard's bane. ANTIDOTE: Castor oil taken immediately, or wine and myrrh, or raisin wine with absinthium, or wormwood decoction. If these are given quickly the patient is saved. This entry provides the earliest systematic documentation of aconite's toxicity and the earliest documented antidote protocols. The cardiac mechanism (aconitine blocks sodium channels, causing heart failure) is consistent with Dioscorides' description of 'chilling.'

label: DMM.B6.6
text: Strychnos (Nightshade family) — στρύχνος — Nightshade (multiple species). Three kinds documented by Dioscorides: (1) the garden kind (Solanum nigrum) — soporific but not as dangerous; (2) the mad kind (possibly Datura or belladonna) — causes madness and hallucinations; (3) the killing kind (Atropa belladonna or Hyoscyamus) — lethal in excess. The Solanaceae family — henbane, mandrake, belladonna, Datura, Solanum — produced more pharmaceutical compounds than any other plant family in the archive. Dioscorides' classification of three kinds of nightshade represents the first systematic attempt to distinguish the pharmacological gradations within a toxic plant genus.

label: DMM.B6.7
text: Antirrhinon (Antidotes — general principles) — ἀντίδοτα — Antidotes and their general principles. Dioscorides closes Book VI with general principles of antidote treatment: (1) Emetics first — get the poison out quickly; (2) Warm wine given after emetic — warms the cold poisons; (3) Opposite treatment to the poison's action — cold poisons treated with warmth; hot poisons with cooling; (4) Movement — keeping the patient active delays absorption of some poisons. These principles anticipate modern toxicology's approach to poison management: decontamination, antidotes, supportive care. The Greek/Roman development of systematic toxicology (Nicander of Colophon, 2nd c. BCE; Dioscorides, 77 CE) represents the first systematic pharmacological science.
